metadata.summary: DDD Anti-Corruption Layer: adapter class in the infrastructure layer that translates between external/legacy API models and your domain model, preventing foreign concepts from leaking in.

content.title: DDD Anti-Corruption Layer: Translation Adapters for External Systems
content.summary: The Anti-Corruption Layer (ACL) is an adapter in the infrastructure layer that translates between the external system's model (a legacy API, a third-party service, another Bounded Context) and your domain model. The domain defines an interface (InventoryChecker, PaymentGateway) that speaks the domain's Ubiquitous Language. The ACL implementation translates to and from the external system's terminology, data shapes, and error types — preventing the external model from leaking into your domain.

why.p: External systems and legacy APIs use terminology, data shapes, and error models that differ from your domain. Without an ACL, their concepts leak directly into your domain objects: an Order starts using the external inventory system's "SKU" and "availableUnits" instead of the domain's "product_id" and "quantity". When the external API changes, you must update domain code.

The ACL eliminates this coupling by concentrating all translation in one place. If the external API changes its field names or response format, only the ACL changes. The domain interface, domain objects, and all domain tests remain unchanged. The ACL also translates errors: an external ExternalApiError becomes a domain InventoryUnavailableError with domain semantics.

list.item: Integrating with any external or third-party API (payment gateways, shipping carriers, inventory services, CRMs).
Integrating with a legacy system that uses a different model or Ubiquitous Language than your bounded context.
Integrating with another Bounded Context in the same organization that has its own model — the ACL prevents model contamination across context boundaries.
Any integration where you want to isolate your domain from external API versioning and breaking changes.
The external system's model is identical to your domain model and you control both — a direct call is simpler.
Throwaway integrations that will be replaced within a sprint — overhead exceeds value for very short-lived adapters.
The integration is purely read-only and the external data maps trivially to a DTO used only in the application layer (not in the domain).

rule.statement: The domain layer MUST define an interface for the external dependency (e.g., InventoryChecker, PaymentGateway) using only domain types and domain terminology. The ACL implementation in the infrastructure layer MUST implement this interface and be the ONLY place that imports the external client SDK or legacy API types.
rule.rationale: Domain code importing external SDK types is a direct coupling: SDK upgrades, API changes, and provider swaps require domain code changes. The interface + ACL pattern confines that coupling to the infrastructure layer.

rule.statement: The ACL MUST translate external error types into domain exceptions. An ExternalApiError from the inventory service MUST become InventoryUnavailableError (a domain concept) before it crosses the ACL boundary. Raw external exceptions MUST NOT propagate into domain or application layer code.
rule.rationale: External error types leak external semantics into domain error handling. Domain code should reason about domain outcomes (inventory unavailable) not external infrastructure outcomes (HTTP 503).

rule.statement: The ACL MUST define a fail-safe default for non-critical integrations (e.g., if the inventory API is unreachable, assume available rather than crashing the order flow). Document the failure mode explicitly in the ACL implementation.
rule.rationale: External services are unreliable. Domain flows should degrade gracefully rather than propagate transient infrastructure errors as hard domain failures.

example.title: InventoryChecker ACL for external inventory API
example.code:
# order/domain/interfaces.py -- domain layer, no external imports from abc import ABC, abstractmethod from uuid import UUID
class InventoryChecker(ABC): """Domain interface for inventory availability checks."""
@abstractmethod async def check_availability(self, product_id: UUID, quantity: int) -> bool: """Return True if product is available in required quantity.""" pass
@abstractmethod async def reserve(self, product_id: UUID, quantity: int) -> str: """Reserve inventory. Returns reservation reference.""" pass
# order/infrastructure/acl/inventory_adapter.py -- infrastructure layer from uuid import UUID
from order.domain.interfaces import InventoryChecker from external.inventory_api import InventoryApiClient, ExternalApiError
class InventoryAdapter(InventoryChecker): """Anti-corruption layer for external inventory service."""
def __init__(self, client: InventoryApiClient): self._client = client
async def check_availability(self, product_id: UUID, quantity: int) -> bool: """Check if product is available in required quantity.""" try: # External API uses different terminology response = await self._client.get_stock_level(str(product_id))
# Translate external model to our domain concepts available_quantity = response.get("availableUnits", 0) return available_quantity >= quantity
except ExternalApiError: # Fail safely -- assume available; order flow must not be blocked by # transient inventory API outages. Inventory will catch discrepancies later. return True
async def reserve(self, product_id: UUID, quantity: int) -> str: """Reserve inventory for order.""" try: response = await self._client.create_reservation({ "sku": str(product_id), # External uses "sku"; domain uses product_id "units": quantity, # External uses "units"; domain uses quantity "type": "CUSTOMER_ORDER", }) return response["reservationId"] # Translate to domain reservation reference except ExternalApiError as e: raise InventoryReservationError( f"Could not reserve {quantity} units of {product_id}" ) from e
example.note: The domain only sees InventoryChecker with domain vocabulary (product_id, quantity). The ACL translates to the external API's vocabulary (sku, units, availableUnits). ExternalApiError never crosses the boundary.

example.title: External model leaking into domain Aggregate
example.code:
# BAD: Order aggregate importing external SDK and using external terminology from external.inventory_api import InventoryApiClient
class Order: async def place(self, client: InventoryApiClient): # External SDK imported directly into domain # External terminology ("sku", "availableUnits") leaking in for line in self.lines: response = await client.get_stock_level(str(line.product_id)) if response["availableUnits"] < line.quantity: raise Error("Insufficient inventory") self.status = OrderStatus.PLACED
example.note: InventoryApiClient import in the domain couples the domain to the external SDK version. "availableUnits" is external terminology, not domain vocabulary. Any API change breaks the Aggregate.

section.name: domain-service-usage
section.p: Domain Services are the typical consumer of ACL interfaces. A Domain Service holds pure domain logic that spans multiple entities or requires external data, but it depends on the domain interface, not the ACL implementation.

example.title: Domain service using ACL interface

section.name: tools-and-services
section.p: Tools and services useful for ACL and cross-context integration in DDD systems.

list.item: mermaid-cli (mmdc): render Bounded Context maps from Markdown — npm i -g @mermaid-js/mermaid-cli
context-mapper: DSL for Bounded Context maps and context relationship analysis — https://contextmapper.org
pact-broker / pactflow: consumer-driven contract testing across Bounded Contexts — https://pactflow.io
archunit (Java), dependency-cruiser (TS): enforce layer and Bounded Context boundaries in code — https://www.archunit.org
tach (Python), nx (TS monorepo): module-boundary enforcement — https://github.com/gauge-sh/tach
Kafka / Redpanda + Outbox pattern: Domain Events across contexts with at-least-once delivery; pair with debezium for CDC-based outbox relay.
Temporal: long-running domain workflows (sagas) that span multiple Bounded Contexts.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: DDD ACL work is well-suited to a dedicated subagent that generates adapters from domain interface specifications.
section.prompt:
Phase 1 (modeler): Domain: subscription billing. Produce: - Bounded Contexts (Subscription, Billing, Notifications) with relationships (Customer-Supplier, Shared Kernel, ACL) - Aggregates per context with root + invariants list - Domain events crossing contexts - Ubiquitous Language glossary (term -> definition) Output as Markdown for human review. STOP. Do not write code.
Phase 2 (acl-builder, after sign-off): Generate Anti-Corruption Layer adapters for each external integration point identified in the locked spec. For each adapter: - Domain interface in domain/interfaces.py (domain types only) - ACL implementation in infrastructure/acl/ (external SDK imports here only) - Unit tests using a mock of the external client - Error translation: map all external exceptions to domain exceptions

section.name: agentic-gotchas
section.p: ACLs are consistently under-built by agents. Common failure modes to check explicitly.

list.item: Agents skip the domain interface and import the external SDK directly in application or domain services — require the ABC interface pattern in the prompt.
External exceptions propagate through the ACL boundary unchanged — require explicit try/except with domain exception re-raise in every ACL method.
ACLs are missing entirely when integrating legacy systems — agents are trained to use direct API calls. Flag any direct external SDK import in non-infrastructure code.

LLMs invent Bounded Contexts that do not reflect actual organizational boundaries — the context map must come from real domain experts; the agent only formalizes it. Human-in-loop checkpoint before any code generation.
Agents add business logic inside ACL adapters (pricing calculations, discount rules) — ACL translates only; domain logic belongs in Domain Services or Aggregates.

checklist.check: Domain interface defined in domain layer with no external SDK imports
ACL implementation lives in infrastructure layer, is the only place with external SDK imports
All external exceptions caught and re-raised as domain exceptions before crossing the boundary
Fail-safe default documented for non-critical integrations
ACL unit tests use a mock of the external client, not the real API
No business logic inside ACL adapters — translation only
Ubiquitous Language glossary documents the mapping between external and domain terminology
